heroOverview: Anonychia is a congenital absence of one or more nails, classified under ICD-10 code Q84.3. It results from a failure of the nail matrix to form during fetal development, producing either complete absence of the nail plate (anonychia) or severe underdevelopment (micronychia) from birth. The condition ranges from isolated absence of a single nail to total anonychia affecting all 20 digits, and may occur alone or as part of wider syndromes such as DOOR syndrome, Coffin-Siris syndrome, or ectodermal dysplasia. Causative variants in the RSPO4 gene, which encodes an R-spondin protein critical to Wnt signalling in nail organogenesis, account for most autosomal-recessive cases of isolated total anonychia. While the condition is rare — precise global incidence is not established — it is permanent and cosmetic and functional management is the focus of care, since no medical therapy regenerates absent nail matrices.

keyStats.prevalence: Precise incidence unknown; isolated autosomal-recessive anonychia due to RSPO4 variants is reported in small case series worldwide
keyStats.demographics: Present at birth; affects males and females equally in autosomal-recessive forms; X-linked forms affect males predominantly
keyStats.avgAge: Congenital — diagnosed at birth or in early infancy
keyStats.globalCases: Rare condition; total burden estimated in the hundreds to low thousands globally based on reported kindreds

definition: Anonychia (ICD-10: Q84.3) is the complete congenital absence of the nail plate on one or more digits, resulting from failure of the nail matrix — the proliferating epithelial tissue at the base of the nail bed — to develop normally during fetal growth. The term is derived from the Greek 'an' (without) and 'onyx' (nail). Micronychia refers to a hypoplastic or markedly reduced nail, and is often grouped with anonychia as both reflect the same underlying developmental failure. Anonychia may be isolated, involving only one or a few nails, or total, affecting all fingernails and toenails. It arises as an autosomal-recessive trait when biallelic loss-of-function variants occur in the RSPO4 gene (chromosome 20p13), which encodes R-spondin-4, a secreted activator of Wnt signalling required for nail plate formation. Dominant single-gene and chromosomal causes also exist. Anonychia is a defining or associated feature of several named genetic syndromes including DOOR syndrome (deafness, onychodystrophy, osteodystrophy, mental retardation), Coffin-Siris syndrome, Turner syndrome, and certain ectodermal dysplasias. Acquired anonychia — from trauma, onychomycosis, severe nail psoriasis, lichen planus, or drugs — is distinct from the congenital form and is not classified under Q84.3. The digits most often affected in isolated autosomal-recessive anonychia are the fingers more than the toes, and the thumbs are frequently spared in partial forms.

typesClassification.description: Absence of all 20 nails without other systemic features; typically autosomal recessive due to biallelic RSPO4 variants, with no associated intellectual or systemic abnormality.
Absence of a specific subset of nails, such as all fingernails sparing the thumbs, or only toenails; may be sporadic or follow dominant inheritance.
Nail absence as one feature of a named syndrome such as DOOR syndrome, Coffin-Siris syndrome, or ectodermal dysplasia; associated features depend on the underlying syndrome.
Markedly reduced but present nails, often classified with anonychia as both represent a spectrum of nail matrix developmental failure.
A specific autosomal-recessive phenotype with total or near-total anonychia, normal intelligence, and no other systemic abnormalities, now linked to RSPO4 mutations.

primarySymptoms: Complete absence of one or more nail plates at birth, visible immediately on examination of the digits.
The nail bed is present but exposed — appearing as a slightly keratinised or smooth skin surface where the nail would normally be.
Fingertip hypersensitivity or tenderness due to the absence of the protective nail plate, which normally guards the distal phalangeal surface.
Difficulty with fine motor tasks requiring nail use, such as picking up small objects, peeling stickers, or opening containers.
Nail folds that are normal in structure but frame an absent plate, distinguishing congenital anonychia from nail loss through infection or trauma.
In total anonychia, all 20 digits are affected, creating a distinctive appearance visible from infancy.
In partial forms, adjacent nails may show micronychia — a severely reduced, dysplastic nail plate — alongside completely absent nails.
Absence of the lunula (white crescent) and cuticle on affected digits, since these structures are extensions of the nail matrix.
In syndromic anonychia, additional features such as hearing loss, developmental delay, or skeletal anomalies may be present alongside absent nails.
Psychosocial distress and self-consciousness, particularly in school-age children and adolescents, related to the visible difference in nail appearance.
earlyWarningSigns: Absence of visible nail tissue on one or more digits at birth, noted on the newborn examination.
Parental observation that an infant's digits lack nails by the first weeks of life.
Failure of any nail tissue to appear or grow during the first year, distinguishing the congenital absence from delay.
Family history of absent or very small nails in parents or siblings, suggesting autosomal-recessive or dominant inheritance.
emergencySigns: Secondary bacterial infection of the exposed nail bed — presenting with redness, swelling, purulent discharge, and tenderness — which requires prompt antibiotic treatment.
Signs of a broader genetic syndrome in a child with anonychia: sensorineural hearing loss, developmental regression, skeletal abnormalities, or seizures, warranting urgent specialist evaluation.
Trauma to the exposed nail bed causing bleeding that does not stop with direct pressure, given the absence of the protective nail plate.

causes.description: Loss-of-function mutations in both copies of RSPO4, which encodes R-spondin-4, a secreted Wnt pathway agonist expressed in the embryonic nail matrix, prevent the molecular signalling cascade required to form the nail plate; the inheritance is autosomal recessive, requiring pathogenic variants from both parents.

causes.cause: Dominant mutations in nail-specific transcription factors
causes.description: Single pathogenic variants in genes regulating ectodermal appendage formation can cause partial or total anonychia in an autosomal-dominant pattern, sometimes within a broader ectodermal dysplasia syndrome.

Structural or numerical chromosomal changes affecting regions harbouring nail development genes can produce anonychia as part of a wider phenotype; Turner syndrome (45,X) and other chromosomal syndromes may present with nail hypoplasia.

causes.cause: Developmental arrest of the nail matrix
causes.description: During weeks 9–20 of fetal development, the nail organ forms from specialised ectoderm at the dorsal digit tips; any insult — genetic, vascular, or teratogenic — that disrupts matrix induction during this critical window can prevent nail formation.

Prenatal exposure to anticonvulsants such as phenytoin has been associated with digit and nail hypoplasia as part of fetal anticonvulsant syndrome; this mechanism is distinct from inherited anonychia.

riskFactors.description: Being the child of two carriers of a pathogenic RSPO4 variant confers a 25% risk per pregnancy of isolated total anonychia; parental consanguinity markedly increases carrier frequency.
First-cousin or closer marriages increase the probability that both parents carry the same recessive RSPO4 variant, substantially raising the offspring's risk of homozygosity for the mutation.

riskFactors.factor: Known family syndrome with nail involvement
riskFactors.description: A parent or sibling with ectodermal dysplasia, DOOR syndrome, or Coffin-Siris syndrome significantly raises the risk of the child inheriting the same or related nail defect.
Phenytoin and some other anticonvulsants taken during the first trimester are associated with a phenotype including nail hypoplasia and digit abnormalities; this is distinct from heritable anonychia.
Chromosomal syndromes that include nail hypoplasia or absence raise the risk; karyotyping and chromosomal microarray are part of the diagnostic workup in syndromic presentations.

affectedDemographics: Neonates and infants of any sex — present at birth in all inherited forms
Children of consanguineous parents in populations where cousin marriage is practised, due to higher rates of RSPO4 homozygosity
Individuals with syndromic forms of anonychia as part of broader genetic conditions
No significant geographic or ethnic predisposition identified, though RSPO4 founder mutations have been described in specific isolated populations
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of congenital anonychia is primarily clinical: absent or severely hypoplastic nail plates present from birth, without a history of nail loss from infection, trauma, or systemic disease, constitute the defining finding. A systematic examination documents which specific nails are affected and whether micronychia is present alongside complete absence. The clinical assessment also looks for associated features — hearing loss, developmental milestones, facial dysmorphia, skeletal anomalies, teeth and hair abnormalities — that could indicate a syndrome. Dermoscopy of the nail bed and proximal nail fold may show an absent or hypoplastic nail matrix crescent. Genetic evaluation is central to confirming the diagnosis and type. Targeted sequencing of RSPO4 confirms the autosomal-recessive isolated form when biallelic pathogenic variants are found. When RSPO4 sequencing is negative or features suggest a syndrome, multi-gene panel sequencing covering ectodermal dysplasia and nail disorder genes is used, or exome sequencing. Chromosomal microarray is indicated when dysmorphia or developmental delay is present. Family members should be offered carrier testing once a variant is identified. There is no prenatal biomarker for anonychia; prenatal ultrasound does not reliably detect isolated nail absence. Histology of the nail bed is rarely needed but shows absent nail matrix when performed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents which nails are absent or hypoplastic, distinguishes congenital from acquired absence, and identifies the pattern (partial, total, or syndromic).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician carefully examines all fingernails and toenails; no needles or procedures are involved — the examination takes a few minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies biallelic pathogenic variants that confirm autosomal-recessive isolated anonychia and provides definitive molecular diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample or saliva swab is taken; DNA is extracted and the RSPO4 gene sequenced; results take 2–6 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used when RSPO4 testing is negative or clinical features suggest a syndrome; covers genes for ectodermal dysplasia, DOOR syndrome, and Coffin-Siris syndrome.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same blood or saliva collection as RSPO4 testing; broader analysis with results in 4–12 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects copy-number variants associated with chromosomal syndromes when developmental delay, dysmorphia, or multi-system involvement is present.
Screens for sensorineural hearing loss, a key feature of DOOR syndrome and some ectodermal dysplasias that co-present with anonychia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-invasive tone or click testing; newborns undergo automated auditory brainstem response testing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates the terminal phalanges for hypoplasia or absence, and screens for osteodystrophy that may accompany syndromic anonychia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Plain X-rays of the hands and feet; a brief, painless procedure.

treatmentOverview: There is no treatment that regenerates an absent nail matrix — once the matrix fails to form, the nail plate cannot develop. Management is therefore focused on protection, function, cosmesis, and genetic counselling. Prosthetic nails, made from acrylic or gel materials customised to fit each digit, are the most practical intervention for children and adults who are bothered by the cosmetic appearance or who have functional difficulty with tasks requiring nails. These prosthetics are attached with medical adhesive and can be shaped and coloured to match natural nails. Physical occupational therapy can help younger children adapt fine-motor strategies that do not depend on nail use. Protective digit covers or soft fingertip protectors help during activities that risk trauma to the exposed nail bed. Psychosocial support and, where appropriate, referral to genetic counselling services for the family, are important components of long-term care. Genetic counselling addresses recurrence risk, carrier testing, and family planning options including preimplantation genetic testing for couples who are both carriers of a pathogenic RSPO4 variant. Syndromic forms require management of associated features by the relevant specialists — audiology for hearing loss in DOOR syndrome, developmental paediatrics for cognitive involvement in Coffin-Siris syndrome, and dermatology for associated skin and hair findings in ectodermal dysplasia.

medicalTreatments.name: Prosthetic nail fitting
medicalTreatments.description: Custom acrylic or gel nail prosthetics are fabricated by a specialist nail technician or podiatrist to fit the shape of each affected digit; they are attached with medical adhesive and replaced as needed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: High patient satisfaction for cosmetic concerns; approximately 80% of individuals report improved quality of life and reduced self-consciousness with prosthetics in observational reports.

medicalTreatments.name: Occupational therapy for fine motor adaptation
medicalTreatments.description: Targeted hand therapy teaches children and adults strategies for tasks that typically rely on nails — opening containers, manipulating small objects, typing — using alternative grip techniques or adaptive tools.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for improving functional independence; most children reach age-appropriate fine motor milestones with targeted therapy within one to two years.

medicalTreatments.name: Protective digit covers
medicalTreatments.description: Soft silicone or fabric finger protectors guard the exposed nail bed during contact sports, manual work, or activities that risk trauma to the sensitive fingertip.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Practical and well tolerated; reduce trauma-related discomfort and secondary infection risk.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical antiseptics for nail bed care
medicalTreatments.description: When the exposed nail bed shows signs of superficial infection or irritation, topical antiseptic solutions or low-potency antibiotic creams are applied to prevent secondary bacterial infection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Standard wound-care practice; effective for preventing and treating superficial nail bed infections in the absence of nail plate protection.

alternativeTreatments.name: Gel nail overlays
alternativeTreatments.description: Nail technicians experienced with nail disorders can apply UV-cured gel overlays directly to the nail bed to create a functional and cosmetically acceptable nail surface without requiring a separate prosthesis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Psychological and peer support
alternativeTreatments.description: Peer support groups connecting individuals with visible differences or rare genetic conditions reduce social anxiety and improve self-acceptance, particularly during adolescence.

whySeeSpecialist: A clinical geneticist or genetic counsellor should be involved early to confirm the molecular cause, provide an accurate recurrence-risk figure for the family, and plan carrier testing for relatives. A dermatologist experienced in nail disorders can guide prosthetic options and manage secondary nail bed complications. Children with syndromic anonychia need multidisciplinary input — audiology, developmental paediatrics, and clinical genetics — coordinated through a specialist centre for rare skin and nail disorders.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a clinical geneticist with experience in ectodermal dysplasias and nail disorders, or a dermatologist who specifically manages genodermatoses. For prosthetic nails, look for a podiatrist or nail technician trained in medical-grade prosthetics for nail absence. Children with syndromic features benefit from a multidisciplinary rare-disease clinic. Ask whether the centre offers genetic counselling as part of their service, including preimplantation genetic testing pathways for families planning future pregnancies.
preventionStrategies: Carrier testing for couples with a family history of autosomal-recessive anonychia before or early in pregnancy to determine reproductive risk.
Preimplantation genetic testing (PGT-M) for couples who are both confirmed RSPO4 carriers who wish to avoid the 25% per-pregnancy risk.
Prenatal genetic counselling if a chromosomal syndrome or known variant is present in a parent.
Avoid unnecessary anticonvulsant teratogen exposure in the first trimester where medically possible, in consultation with the treating neurologist.
Genetic counselling for the extended family of a proband with an identified RSPO4 or other pathogenic variant to identify other carriers.
lifestyleModifications: Use protective gloves or fingertip guards during manual tasks, garden work, or contact sports to shield the exposed nail beds.
Keep fingertip skin well moisturised to prevent dryness and cracking of the unprotected nail bed surface.
Select shoes that give adequate toe space to prevent pressure on toenail beds, which can be tender without the protective nail plate.
Talk openly with school staff about the condition so peers and teachers understand and do not exclude the child from activities.
Access psychological support services if body image or social confidence is significantly affected, particularly during adolescence.

recommended: Balanced diet adequate in vitamins A, B-complex, C, and D, zinc, and iron — nutrients that support skin and epithelial tissue health
Adequate protein intake to maintain skin integrity and wound healing of the nail bed
Staying hydrated to prevent nail bed skin dryness and cracking
avoid: Harsh chemical solvents without protective gloves — these can damage the unprotected nail bed skin more readily than covered nails
Excessive hand washing with harsh soaps that dry and crack the nail bed skin
Biotin supplements without medical advice — not effective for congenitally absent nails and can interfere with thyroid tests

exerciseGuidelines: There are no restrictions on exercise for individuals with anonychia. All sports are accessible, though protective finger guards are advisable during contact sports, rock climbing, or manual activities where fingertip trauma is likely. Swimming is safe and well tolerated. Standard exercise supports general health without any specific nail-related contraindication.
dailyManagement: Apply a thin layer of moisturiser or barrier cream to the nail beds morning and evening to prevent dryness and cracking.
Inspect fingertips weekly for signs of redness, swelling, or discharge that could indicate nail bed infection.
Wear soft-toed footwear that does not press on toenail bed areas.
Keep prosthetic nails clean and replace the adhesive regularly to prevent bacterial build-up underneath.
Schedule genetic review every two to three years during childhood to update the family on any new gene-level findings relevant to their specific variant.
prognosis: Anonychia is a permanent structural condition; the absent nail plates do not grow back because the nail matrix never formed. Life expectancy is entirely normal for isolated anonychia. The condition carries no systemic complications. The practical challenges — reduced fingertip protection and difficulty with nail-dependent fine-motor tasks — are well managed with prosthetics and occupational therapy in the majority of affected individuals. Quality of life is primarily determined by cosmetic acceptance and psychosocial adaptation, which are generally achievable with appropriate support, particularly when diagnosis and counselling occur early. Syndromic forms of anonychia carry the prognosis of the underlying syndrome: DOOR syndrome includes intellectual disability, and Coffin-Siris syndrome ranges from mild to moderate developmental delay. Genetic counselling provides families with accurate risk information for future pregnancies and identifies relatives who are carriers.
recoveryTimeline: Anonychia is lifelong and does not resolve. There is no treatment that induces nail regrowth. The timeline of care is instead about adaptation: prosthetics can be fitted from early childhood and updated as the child grows. Occupational therapy interventions typically run for six to twelve months for children, after which most functional adaptations are incorporated into daily life. Genetic workup, including molecular diagnosis, is usually completed within three to six months of referral. Psychological adjustment is an ongoing process, with support most important during school starting age and adolescence.
complications: Secondary bacterial infection of the exposed nail bed, most commonly with Staphylococcus aureus — presents with redness, swelling, warmth, and pus, and requires topical or oral antibiotics.
Persistent fingertip hypersensitivity and pain from daily minor trauma to the unprotected nail bed, which can limit participation in manual activities.

Psychosocial difficulties — social anxiety, body image concerns, and bullying — particularly during school years; professional psychological support and peer group connection are effective interventions.
Prosthetic nail complications — allergic contact dermatitis to nail adhesive or acrylic components, which requires a change of materials.
Missed diagnosis of an associated syndrome if anonychia is managed in isolation without genetic evaluation, potentially delaying treatment of co-occurring hearing loss or developmental issues.

supportResources.name: NORD — National Organization for Rare Disorders: Anonychia
supportResources.description: Overview of congenital anonychia for patients and families, including diagnostic information and research resources.

supportResources.name: Genetic and Rare Diseases Information Center (GARD)
supportResources.description: NIH resource providing patient-facing information on genetics, diagnosis, and management of congenital anonychia.

supportResources.name: Ectodermal Dysplasia Society
supportResources.description: Support organisation for individuals with ectodermal dysplasias, including forms that involve nail absence, providing peer support and specialist referral guidance.

sources.title: Bergmann C et al. Loss of Nephrocystin-3 Function Can Cause Embryonic Lethality, Joubert-Like Syndrome, Tapetoretinal Degeneration, Endocrine-Exocrine Pancreatic Insufficiency
Blaydon DC et al. The Gene Encoding R-Spondin 4 (RSPO4), a Secreted Protein Implicated in Wnt Signaling, Is Mutated in Inherited Anonychia. Nature Genetics, 2006
de la Fuente-Garcia A et al. Anonychia and polyonychia: review of the literature. Dermatology Reports, 2012
Genetic and Rare Diseases Information Center — Anonychia congenita

confusedWithConditions.name: Onychomycosis (fungal nail infection)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Onychomycosis causes nail thickening, discolouration, and crumbling acquired during life from fungal infection; anonychia is a congenital absence of the nail present from birth without any infectious cause.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nail psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Nail psoriasis can cause nail pitting, oil spots, and separation of the plate from the bed (onycholysis) acquired in the context of psoriatic skin disease; it appears after birth and is not a failure of nail formation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Alopecia (nail involvement)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Alopecia areata can cause nail pitting and trachyonychia as an acquired autoimmune phenomenon; it is not a congenital absence of the nail matrix.

relatedConditions.name: Ectodermal dysplasia
relatedConditions.relevance: Ectodermal dysplasias are a broad group of conditions affecting nails, hair, teeth, and sweat glands; anonychia is a core feature of several ectodermal dysplasia subtypes.

relatedConditions.name: Onychomycosis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common cause of nail loss in the general population — important to distinguish from the congenital absence seen in anonychia.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriasis with nail involvement
relatedConditions.relevance: Psoriatic nail disease produces nail pitting and onycholysis that can superficially resemble nail dystrophy; differentiated from congenital anonychia by its acquired course and skin findings.

relatedConditions.name: Alopecia
relatedConditions.relevance: Alopecia areata can involve nail trachyonychia and pitting as an immune-mediated phenomenon; important to distinguish from the genetic nail absence in anonychia.

faqs.question: What is anonychia?
faqs.answer: Anonychia is the congenital absence of one or more nails from birth, caused by failure of the nail matrix to develop during fetal growth. It ranges from a single missing nail to complete absence of all 20 nails.

faqs.question: Can absent nails grow back in anonychia?
faqs.answer: No. In congenital anonychia the nail matrix — the tissue that generates the nail plate — never formed, so there is nothing from which a nail can grow. The absence is permanent. Prosthetic nails are available for cosmetic and functional reasons.

faqs.question: What gene causes anonychia?
faqs.answer: Most cases of isolated autosomal-recessive total anonychia are caused by loss-of-function variants in the RSPO4 gene on chromosome 20p13. RSPO4 encodes R-spondin-4, a protein that activates the Wnt signalling pathway required for nail matrix formation.

faqs.question: Is anonychia painful?
faqs.answer: The nail beds are often hypersensitive or tender because the protective nail plate is absent, exposing the underlying soft tissue to pressure and temperature. Minor daily activities can cause discomfort. Protective fingertip covers and adapted grip techniques help manage this.

faqs.question: Is anonychia hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes. Isolated total anonychia due to RSPO4 variants is autosomal recessive — a child must inherit one faulty copy from each parent to be affected. Parents are typically unaffected carriers. The risk to each subsequent child of two carriers is 25%.

faqs.question: What syndromes are associated with anonychia?
faqs.answer: Anonychia can occur as part of DOOR syndrome (deafness, onychodystrophy, osteodystrophy, mental retardation), Coffin-Siris syndrome, Turner syndrome, and several ectodermal dysplasias. When nail absence is found alongside other features, evaluation for an underlying syndrome is important.

faqs.question: How is anonychia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical: absent nail plates present from birth without a history of nail loss. Molecular confirmation comes from RSPO4 gene sequencing. When results are negative or other features are present, broader genetic panel testing or exome sequencing is used.

faqs.question: Can prosthetic nails help with anonychia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Custom acrylic or gel prosthetic nails are fitted to match the shape of each digit and attached with medical-grade adhesive. They improve both cosmetic appearance and some functional tasks that rely on nail use. About 80% of users report improved quality of life with prosthetics.

faqs.question: What activities are limited by anonychia?
faqs.answer: Tasks that typically use nails — picking up small items, peeling labels, scratching surfaces — can be difficult. Occupational therapy teaches adaptive techniques. Contact sports and manual work require finger protection to prevent injury to exposed nail beds. Most other activities are unrestricted.

faqs.question: Is anonychia dangerous?
faqs.answer: Isolated anonychia does not affect life expectancy or internal organ function. The main risks are secondary nail bed infections from minor injuries and the psychosocial impact of visible difference. Syndromic forms carry the risks of the underlying genetic condition.

faqs.question: What is the difference between anonychia and onychomycosis?
faqs.answer: Anonychia is a congenital absence of the nail present from birth due to a genetic defect. Onychomycosis is an acquired fungal infection that damages existing nails during life, causing thickening, discolouration, and crumbling — it does not prevent nail formation.

faqs.question: Does anonychia affect intelligence?
faqs.answer: Isolated anonychia due to RSPO4 mutations does not affect intelligence — affected individuals have entirely normal cognitive development. Syndromic forms, such as DOOR syndrome or Coffin-Siris syndrome, can involve intellectual disability depending on the underlying condition.

faqs.question: What should I do if my newborn has missing nails?
faqs.answer: Tell your paediatrician, who will refer to a clinical geneticist. A systematic examination documents which nails are absent, and genetic testing for RSPO4 and related genes is arranged. Hearing and developmental assessment is also carried out to check for associated syndromes.

faqs.question: Can anonychia be detected before birth?
faqs.answer: Prenatal ultrasound does not reliably detect absent nails. Anonychia is typically diagnosed at birth. If a family has a known RSPO4 variant, preimplantation genetic testing during IVF can identify unaffected embryos before pregnancy begins.

faqs.question: How do I prevent nail bed infections with anonychia?
faqs.answer: Keep the exposed nail beds clean and moisturised. Wear protective gloves or fingertip guards during activities that risk trauma. Inspect the fingertips regularly for redness or swelling. Seek medical attention promptly if infection signs appear, as the unprotected nail bed is more susceptible than skin covered by a nail plate.

faqs.question: Can preimplantation genetic testing prevent anonychia?
faqs.answer: Yes, for couples where both partners are confirmed RSPO4 carriers. Preimplantation genetic testing for monogenic conditions (PGT-M) during IVF can identify embryos that are homozygous for the variant — and only unaffected embryos are transferred.

faqs.question: Is there any research into nail regeneration for anonychia?
faqs.answer: Wnt pathway activators and stem-cell based approaches are being researched for nail and hair regeneration in laboratory settings. No clinical treatment is currently available to regenerate a congenitally absent nail matrix, but the field is advancing.

faqs.question: How does anonychia affect quality of life?
faqs.answer: Quality of life is primarily affected by cosmetic visibility and minor functional limitations. Most people with isolated anonychia adapt well, particularly with prosthetics and therapy. Psychosocial impact is greatest during school years; peer support and psychological counselling significantly improve self-acceptance and daily function.

faqs.question: Do prosthetic nails for anonychia need a doctor's prescription?
faqs.answer: A prosthetic nail fitting does not require a prescription, but medical referral to a specialist podiatrist or nail prosthetic service is the best route to ensure proper fit, appropriate materials, and avoidance of allergic adhesives. Your dermatologist or geneticist can provide a referral.

faqs.question: Is anonychia covered by insurance?
faqs.answer: Coverage varies by insurance plan and country. Prosthetic nails for medical reasons and occupational therapy for functional impairment are often partially reimbursable. Genetic testing for a confirmed hereditary condition is generally covered. Check with your insurer and provide documentation of the congenital diagnosis.

metaDescription: Anonychia is a congenital absence of nails caused by RSPO4 gene variants. Learn symptoms, genetic diagnosis, prosthetic options, and long-term management.
